food_subgroup: Breaded fish with rice and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for breaded fish rice bowl with sweet chilli sauce. FOODON_00002764 (fish dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers breaded/battered cooked fish as the primary component. The full bowl (rice, sauce, egg) is not represented by a single FoodEx2 code; closest_parent applied to the dominant protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (fish dish) is the closest available parent. No specific code for breaded fish rice bowl with Thai chilli sauce exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 420 g, comprising roughly 120 g breaded fish, 200 g butter rice, 60 g sweet Thai chilli sauce, and one sous vide egg (~60 g).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Breaded fish fillet (SR Legacy 15264), Cooked white rice (SR Legacy 20045), Sweet chilli sauce (Branded Foods), Butter (SR Legacy 01001), Whole egg cooked (SR Legacy 01129)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai Chilli Fish Bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted-average composition of the five primary components at their approximate proportions in the bowl: breaded fish fillet 29%, butter rice 48%, sweet Thai chilli sauce 14%, butter 2%, sous vide egg 7%. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Branded Foods entries used for each component. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice GI reference and breaded fish composite analogy
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai Chilli Fish Bowl. GI estimated at 62 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: white jasmine rice (GI ~64–68, University of Sydney GI Database) weighted against the sweet Thai chilli sauce contribution (high-sugar sauce, GI ~55 analogous to sweet chilli condiment). Breaded fish and egg contribute negligible glycemic load. Composite GI of 62 (medium) is a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (62 × 94.1) / 100 ≈ 58.3, rounded to 58.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full serving (~420 g) delivers approximately 1,302 mg sodium, representing over 56% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl and request reduced-sodium chilli sauce, as the sweet Thai chilli sauce and breading are the primary sodium contributors in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) driven by white butter rice and sweet Thai chilli sauce; a full serving provides approximately 94 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~58.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice where available, and be aware that the sweet Thai chilli sauce adds approximately 15–20 g of free sugars per serving, which will accelerate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and egg (~1.8 g per 100 g; ~7.6 g per serving) and elevated sodium (~1,302 mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; deep-frying of the fish adds oxidised lipids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request grilled rather than breaded fish where possible, and pair with a vegetable side to improve the overall dietary pattern of the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Chilli Fish Bowl is a Western-Asian fusion rice bowl dish served in Singapore cafes and casual dining establishments. It consists of a deep-fried breaded white fish fillet — typically dory or basa — served over a portion of butter-enriched steamed white rice, drizzled generously with sweet Thai chilli sauce, and accompanied by a sous vide egg. The sous vide egg is cooked at low temperature (approximately 63–65°C) to produce a custard-like, jammy yolk with a barely-set white, adding richness and protein to the bowl. The flavour profile combines the crispy, savoury crunch of the breaded fish with the sweet, mildly spicy, and tangy character of the Thai chilli sauce, balanced by the buttery, slightly sticky rice and the creamy egg. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~706 kcal per full serving) with a reasonable protein contribution (~33 g per serving) from the fish and egg, but is notable for its moderate-to-high carbohydrate load (~94 g per serving) from the white rice and sweet sauce, elevated sodium (~1,302 mg per serving) from the sauce and breading, and moderate saturated fat from butter and egg. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to the wheat-based breadcrumbs, and contains fish, egg, dairy, and potential soy allergens. It is commonly found on cafe menus in Singapore, particularly in Western-style coffee shops and food courts.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai Chilli Fish Bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of five primary components using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Branded Foods entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (white jasmine rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the proportions are based on a realistic serving description.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no direct HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific dish. 2. The cuisine field in the input JSON was listed as 'Coffee' (likely reflecting the cafe category tag from GrabFood), which has been corrected to 'Western-Asian Fusion' based on the dish description. 3. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this dish does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the processing date. 4. Halal status is marked true based on the dish components (fish, egg, rice, butter, chilli sauce) but cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level halal certification; this should be verified against The Tree Cafe - Funan's certification status. 5. Sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA component data and are not -1; all numeric values are present. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all micronutrients were estimable from USDA component data. 6. The dish is served by one restaurant (The Tree Cafe - Funan) on GrabFood Singapore at SGD 12.50.
